nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline of northwestern Lake Tanganyika; typically found hugging rock faces and crevices in the littoral zone

decorPreferences: Extensive rock piles and cave formations for shelter and spawning
PVC tubes or clay rockwork as spawning sites

diet.notes: Tends to accept high-quality cichlid pellets, frozen foods (mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, cyclops), and spirulina-enriched foods. Vegetable matter such as blanched spinach or spirulina flakes is generally recommended alongside meaty foods. Does not typically surface-feed; sinking or mid-water foods are preferred.

goodWith: Other Tanganyikan cichlids of similar size (Altolamprologus, Cyprichromis, Synodontis catfish)
Shellies (Lamprologus ocellatus, Neolamprologus multifasciatus) in sufficiently large tanks
avoidWith: Conspecifics outside of an established pair (highly aggressive toward unpaired conspecifics)
Other Julidochromis species (hybridization risk and aggression)
Mbuna-type cichlids (incompatible water chemistry and aggression profile)
Small, timid fish that may be harassed

summary: Marlier's Julie is a boldly patterned cichlid endemic to the rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika. The species forms stable, long-term pair bonds and is widely considered manageable as a bonded pair, though it tends to be aggressive toward unpaired conspecifics introduced to its territory.
careNotes:
Marlier's Julie (Julidochromis marlieri) is one of the larger members of its genus, reaching up to 15 cm, and is endemic to the northwestern rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika. Hard, alkaline rift-lake chemistry is essential — soft or acidic tap water is unsuitable without buffering to replicate Tanganyikan conditions (pH 7.8–9.0, gH 10–25, kH 8–20).

Around four to five Julidochromis species are found in the aquarium trade (marlieri, transcriptus, ornatus, regani, and dickfeldi), and cross-species hybridization is possible and common. Best practice is to keep only one species per tank or system to avoid producing unintentional hybrids.

Marlier's Julie forms stable pair bonds that tend to endure long-term, and bonded pairs engage in shared biparental brood care. Unpaired individuals — or any fish introduced from outside the established pair — are typically treated with significant aggression. Introducing new fish to a resident pair's territory can result in injury or death. A tank of at least 150 L with abundant rockwork is broadly recommended for a pair; larger tanks are needed if other Tanganyikan species are to be added.

Marlier's Julie characteristically swims close to rock surfaces regardless of orientation — the fish can often be seen hugging vertical or even overhanging rock faces. Rock piles with caves, PVC tubes, or clay structures should be provided as both territory anchors and spawning sites. Large water changes should be avoided with an established, spawning pair, as sudden chemistry shifts can disrupt breeding activity.

The species occupies rocky substrate at depths of 3–9 m in the wild and is considered endemic to the northwestern portion of the lake, spanning the border region of the Democratic Republic of Congo, Tanzania, and Burundi. Several geographic color variants exist; the classic marlieri pattern is a white body with dark-brown to black checkerboard markings.
breedingNotes: Breeding is generally considered easy for an established, bonded pair. Pairs spawn secretively inside caves or crevices, with females typically depositing 50–100 eggs on cave walls or rocky surfaces. Both parents guard eggs and fry (biparental cave-spawning cichlid). Fry are mobile shortly after hatching and accept brine shrimp nauplii from day one. Brood care tends to be extended — fry often remain in the parents' territory until they reach approximately 2–3 cm. The main challenge is forming a compatible pair; purchasing a group of 4–6 juveniles and allowing natural pairing is commonly recommended.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op (404 on all tested URL patterns). SeriouslyFish provides comprehensive species-specific husbandry data for this taxon.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (max 13 cm TL vs SeriouslyFish 15 cm — averaged to min 8 cm, max 15 cm range reflecting hobbyist and wild data), temperature (FishBase 22–25°C vs SeriouslyFish 23–27°C — used 23–27°C as the hobby-consensus range), pH (both agree 7.5–9.0; used 7.8 as practical Tanganyika floor per trade convention), depth 3–9 m, and taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes confirmed). Hardness: FishBase reports 3–9 dH (wild measurement) vs hobby standard 10–25 dH; used hobby parameters aligned with Tanganyika aquarium practice. Frontosa entry cross-referenced for Tanganyika parameter consensus.

fish.breedingNotes: Marlier's Julie is a cave-spawning species in which both parents share brood care. Pairs spawn on cave walls or rocky crevice surfaces and typically produce clutches of 50–100 eggs. Brood care tends to be extended, with fry often remaining in the parents' territory until they reach approximately 2–3 cm. Forming a compatible pair is generally considered the main challenge; purchasing a group of four to six juveniles and allowing natural pairing is the commonly recommended approach.
